entry.title: From Agent to MCP: What Building an MCP Taught Me About the Future of AI Integration
entry.content:
1. The moment that sparked the question

I've been building automations for a few years now. Not because someone asked me to — because I genuinely wanted to see if it worked.

It started with RPA. And honestly, for what it was, it worked. It solved real problems. But every time a process changed, the bot broke. Every time something shifted upstream, I was back in the designer fixing selectors and re-mapping fields. It was useful — just fragile in a way that bothered me.

Then I added LLMs to the mix. Suddenly the automation could handle variability, could read a document instead of just clicking through a form. That was exciting. But now I had two systems with completely different failure modes sitting next to each other, and I was the glue.

So I went further. I built a custom agent from scratch — a proper one, with skills, context management, decision logic. I wrote about it here. That project taught me more about how AI systems actually behave than anything I'd read. And when it worked, it really worked.

But there's always a next thing to build.

When Joule Work Desktop was announced at Sapphire, MCP was the integration standard. I didn't overthink it — if I wanted to connect to Joule, that was the path. What I didn't expect was how much building an MCP would make me question everything I thought I already understood.

The surface looks familiar: you're still giving a model tools to call, still thinking about context, still asking yourself what the model needs to know to be useful. But the architecture underneath is different in ways that matter. And those differences aren't obvious until you're in the middle of building.

This post is about what I found there.

2. Two ways to give an LLM context: Agents with Skills vs. MCP

Both approaches solve the same fundamental problem: the model doesn't know anything about your system unless you tell it.

You define what tools exist. You describe what they do. The model decides when and how to use them. That mechanic is identical in both. If you've built one, the mental model for the other isn't foreign. You're not starting from zero.

That's where the similarity ends — and where it gets interesting.

What surprised me about the agent

I'll be honest: I went into the agent build fully expecting to suffer.

Years of writing integrations the hard way had trained me well. I had my mental checklist ready: parse the API response, map the fields, pass the values explicitly between steps, handle the edge cases nobody documented. I was prepared for a week of plumbing before I could write a single line of actual logic.

Then I typed "for each result, do this" — and the agent just... did it. A proper for-each. No code. No explicit loop. I called an API and the response landed in context already mapped, already interpretable, ready to use in the next step. The model looked at the shape of the data and figured it out.

I sat there for a second genuinely unsure if it had worked or if I was about to discover a very elegant failure.

It had worked.

That moment reset a lot of assumptions I didn't know I was carrying. The plumbing I had prepared to build never needed to exist. What was left was only the part that actually required my brain: the business rules, the scoring logic, the decision criteria. The stuff that actually matters.

What's different about MCP

With the custom agent, I was still the director. The agent had skills, the skills called APIs, I defined the flow, I could see everything happening. It was my system behaving the way I told it to.

MCP is a different mental model — and it took me a moment to fully land on it.

You build a server. You expose tools. And then you let go.

The client — in this case Joule Desktop — connects to your server, discovers what you've built, and decides how to use it based on what the user asks. You don't orchestrate the flow. You don't manage the context. You define the capability and the client figures out the rest.

The first time I really internalized that, my instinct was mild panic. What do you mean I don't control the flow? But then something clicked: I wasn't building an agent anymore. I was building an interface. A set of capabilities that any MCP-compatible client could plug into — today Joule, tomorrow something else entirely. That's not losing control. That's actually a better kind of architecture.

The real difference: who's in charge

  Custom Agent (Skills) MCP

Who orchestratesThe agentThe client (Joule, Claude, etc.)
Where context livesInside the agentInside the client
Who can use the toolsOnly that agentAny MCP-compatible client
DeploymentPlatform-boundIndependent server
What you controlEverythingThe tools and their outputs

Neither is better. The agent gave me more control and visibility — which was exactly right for a workflow that needed precise, predictable steps. MCP gave me portability and decoupling — which was exactly right for something I wanted any compatible client to use without rebuilding from scratch.

The question isn't which one is superior. The question is what you're actually trying to build.

 

3. What surprised me while building

The design changed three times. And that was the real lesson.

When I start a new server, my brain goes straight to the same place it always does: define the endpoints, figure out the data model, write the code. I've done it enough times that it's almost automatic.

That instinct completely failed me here.

The first version had too many tools, each doing too little. Classic API thinking — small, focused, composable. The kind of thing that looks great in a diagram and makes you feel like a responsible adult. The model looked at my beautiful diagram and chose the wrong tool anyway. Every time. It was combining things I hadn't anticipated, skipping steps that seemed obvious, and generally behaving like it had never seen my very clean diagram. Because it hadn't. Because it doesn't work that way.

I went back in. Second version: fewer tools, each doing more. The model seemed happier. I was not. I now had tools that behaved differently depending on the input, and I couldn't clearly explain what they did even to myself. That's usually a sign something has gone wrong.

Third version — something finally clicked. And what changed wasn't the code. It was how I was thinking about the whole thing.

You're not designing endpoints for a developer to call. You're designing capabilities for a model to reason about. The name you give a tool shapes when the model reaches for it. The description isn't documentation — it's an instruction. You're writing for a different kind of reader, and once that landed for me, the design stopped fighting back.

A rule I wish someone had told me on day one: don't start building a tool until you can describe it in one sentence to a non-developer. If you can't explain it clearly, the model won't figure it out either. Write the descriptions before you write the code. Yes, before.

Then I ran into the actual hard part. And it wasn't the server.

The server was running. The tools were working. I was feeling pretty good about myself.

And then someone asked a real question and the answer came back technically correct, well-structured, and completely useless.

That's the failure mode nobody puts in the tutorial. A model without the right context doesn't throw an error — it generates a confident, beautifully formatted response that says absolutely nothing of value. It sounds like it knows what it's talking about. It doesn't. And that's scarier than a stack trace because at least a stack trace tells you something is broken.

I ended up spending more time going back to fix the knowledge layer than I'd spent building the server in the first place. Cleaning structure, rethinking what actually needed to be there versus what was just noise dressed up as content. All work that would have been faster — and less humbling — if I'd treated it as the first problem to solve instead of the thing I'd get to eventually.

The lesson, painfully earned: LLMs don't create knowledge. They surface it. Put noise in, get noise back — in a very professional tone. The knowledge layer is the foundation. The server is just the delivery mechanism. I had those two things backwards for longer than I'd like to admit.

The part I didn't expect to enjoy: vector search

I'll be honest — before this project, "embeddings" was one of those words I nodded at confidently without fully understanding.

The vector search itself went smoother than expected. Once it was running it felt almost like cheating — you send a plain question in natural language and the right content comes back ranked by actual meaning, not keyword overlap. No brittle query strings. No AND/OR logic. Just... the right stuff, most of the time.

But getting there sent me down a rabbit hole I had not budgeted for. What embeddings actually are. Why the model you use to generate them changes what comes back. Why chunking strategy — how you split and index the source content — matters way more than it sounds like it should. Why two distance metrics that seem equivalent can return completely different results.

I didn't plan for any of that. I came out of it genuinely glad I went in — and slightly annoyed that nobody had handed me a map.

4. MCP on SAP BTP: the trust boundary as a feature

There's a version of this that lives somewhere else. A different cloud provider, external APIs for embeddings, a third-party vector database. It would have worked technically.

I didn't build that version.

The decision to run the MCP on SAP BTP Cloud Foundry wasn't just about where to host something. It was about where the knowledge base lives — and what that means when you're building inside an enterprise ecosystem.

The institutional knowledge powering the server comes from SAP sources. Running on BTP meant that authentication, connectivity, and data access were all handled through mechanisms that already existed and were already trusted. No external service credentials to manage. No custom connectors to build. No explaining to anyone why sensitive knowledge was touching a third-party API.

That last one matters more than it sounds.

In enterprise settings, the conversation about data governance can easily become longer than the conversation about the actual product. Running inside the platform's trust boundary meant that conversation was mostly resolved before it started. The data doesn't leave the ecosystem. Embeddings are generated through services already inside it. The vector search runs natively. The MCP sits on Cloud Foundry. It's the same platform all the way through — and in this context, that's a feature, not a constraint.

Using the same platform as both a data store and a vector engine was the piece that made the architecture feel coherent. I wasn't stitching together the best individual tool for each layer and hoping they'd talk to each other cleanly. One platform, one trust model, one less thing to explain to anyone who asked.

The boundary wasn't something I worked around. It was something I designed with.

5. Under the hood

I want to keep this section honest and specific. Not a sales pitch for any particular stack — just the actual pieces, how they connect, and the decisions that shaped them. 

Design

The server itself

The MCP is built in Python using FastMCP — the high-level wrapper from the official MCP SDK. If you've never used it, the experience is surprisingly close to writing a FastAPI app: you define functions, decorate them with @McP.tool(), write a proper docstring, and that's your tool. The docstring isn't optional ceremony — it's what the model reads to decide when and how to call the function. Treat it like an API contract written for a reader who can't ask clarifying questions.

@mcp.tool()
def retrieve_similar_cases(query: str, category: str, top_k: int = 3) -> str:
    """
    Retrieve similar historical cases from the vector store.
    Returns expert-validated signals: category, effort, timeline, impact.
    Use these as directional hints — NOT as the final answer.
    """
    ...

The vector search

This is where the retrieval happens. The core is a HANA Cloud query using COSINE_SIMILARITY against a REAL_VECTOR column — filtered by a dimension, ordered by semantic proximity:

SELECT TOP 3 *
FROM KNOWLEDGE_TABLE
WHERE CATEGORY = ?
ORDER BY COSINE_SIMILARITY(EMBEDDING, TO_REAL_VECTOR(?)) DESC

Before this query runs, the incoming text gets converted into an embedding vector via SAP AI Core. That vector is what gets compared against the stored embeddings in HANA. The model that generates the embeddings matters — consistency between ingestion time and query time is non-negotiable. If you embed your knowledge base with model A and query with model B, the cosine similarities will be meaningless. Same model, both directions, always.

For batch processing, the searches run in parallel using ThreadPoolExecutor — one connection per thread, all results collected before returning. The difference in latency between sequential and parallel retrieval across a large batch is significant enough that this wasn't optional.

Two vector indexes, one query pattern

The knowledge base has two separate tables, each with its own EMBEDDING column and its own filter logic. Both are queried using the same cosine similarity pattern, just with different filters and column mappings. The retrieval function runs both searches concurrently and returns a unified result keyed by source type:

with concurrent.futures.ThreadPoolExecutor(max_workers=len(source_types)) as pool:
    results = dict(pool.map(_search_one, source_types))

Simple, but effective. The model gets both result sets in a single tool call and uses them independently when synthesizing the response.

The transport layer on BTP

On Cloud Foundry, the server runs as a standard Python app bound to a port via the $PORT environment variable. No custom buildpack, no containers — just a Procfile, a manifest.yml, and the app itself. The MCP endpoint is exposed over HTTPS at the app's route, which is what Joule Desktop connects to. Authentication and routing are handled by BTP's built-in infrastructure.

The only thing worth calling out: stateless_http=True in the FastMCP init. MCP sessions over HTTP are stateless by design here — each request carries its own context. That's the right model for a server that multiple clients might connect to simultaneously, and it's what made the BTP deployment straightforward without needing session affinity or sticky routing.

6. Where MCP fits in the SAP AI ecosystem

I want to be careful here not to oversell something I'm still learning. But I also think there's something real happening and it's worth saying out loud.

MCP is gaining momentum inside the SAP ecosystem faster than most integration standards do. And I think the reason is simpler than it seems: it solves a problem that everyone building with AI on SAP has been quietly struggling with — how do you give a model structured access to enterprise capabilities without building a custom integration every single time?

Skills were a good answer. They worked. But they were tightly coupled to a specific agent, on a specific platform, for a specific use case. Every new scenario meant starting over.

MCP decouples that. You build the capability once. Any compatible client can use it. Today that's Joule Desktop. Tomorrow it'll be whatever comes after Joule Desktop, and the MCP you built won't care. That's the kind of architectural bet that tends to age well.

Joule Work Desktop is already in pilot. MCP is the integration path they chose. That's not a coincidence — that's a direction. And the developers who understand how to build MCPs well right now, before it's mainstream, before there are established patterns and ready-made templates for everything, are going to have a significant head start on everyone who waits until it's the obvious thing to do.

I'm not saying drop everything and rewrite your agents as MCPs. I'm saying: if you've been curious, now is a genuinely good time to build something small and see what you learn. The protocol is approachable. The tooling is improving fast. And the mental shift it forces — from thinking about functions to thinking about capabilities a model can reason about — is one of those shifts that changes how you see the whole problem space, not just this one project.

Build something. Even if it's small. Especially if it's small.

The best way to understand what MCP actually changes is to be in the middle of building with it and suddenly realize your old instincts aren't working anymore. That moment of friction is where the learning is. Don't skip it by reading about it — go find it.

entry.title: Clarification on Datasphere Live Connection Scope in SAC
entry.content:
Dear SAP Team,
We have created a live connection from SAP Analytics Cloud (SAC) to our SAP Datasphere tenant successfully.
However, we would like to check if it is possible to create a Datasphere live connection in SAC that is restricted to a specific Datasphere space only, instead of the entire tenant.
Our requirement is to use a single Datasphere tenant as multiple environments. For example:

One space is designated for QA/UAT
Another space is used for Performance Testing

We are looking for a way to establish separate SAC live connections for each space to ensure proper isolation between these environments.
Could you please confirm whether this is supported, or suggest the recommended approach to achieve this requirement?

Regards,

China Nageswararao Kalava

entry.title: Re: Zustellung SAP
entry.content:
Regarding this issue, I'd like to confirm with you whether you have logged into the Expert Search application？ Because the email notification feature for this KBA/Note notification requires an S-user to log into the Expert Search application and use it in order to be activated. 

This issue may also you haven't accessed for this app for a long time, or at least more than 365 days. So you need reactive it manually. If you haven't access it for over 1 year, then the system will not send you email notification. So you need click it and set Expert Search application App in SAP for Me under My Home

Regarding to the detail steps about setting in Expert Search application, please kindly check KBA 2478289 - How to get notifications for favorite SAP Notes or KBAs (set up expert search filters) 

After completing the configuration, please monitor for a while if you can receive the email notification about SAP Note/KBA.

entry.title: Re: Exporting validation rule by rule status
entry.content:
Hi colleague, there's no way to filter rules by rule status for downloading.

But if you want to download only active rules, normally inactive rules will be removed from the rule group manually. you can download the rules by downloading the rule groups together with the rules. 

Inactive and draft rules are not expected to be validated with the rule group.

 

Regards,

Ody

entry.title: Re: What is clean core anyway?
entry.content:
@Rajen_Patel Great explanation. 

For me personally, as a consultant & application manager now trained in SAP S/4HANA Public Cloud, I see the clean core approach as a real career advantage.

Since I’ve built a solid understanding of the S/4HANA Public Cloud fundamentals, I feel confident working with different systems in the coming years. As long as they follow clean core principles, especially leveraging key user extensibility, I know I can quickly understand, adapt, and continuously improve these systems.

In that sense, clean core is not just a technical concept, but also an enabler for flexibility, scalability, and long-term professional growth 😊

Best regards,

Kerisha

entry.title: June Developer Challenge - CAP Knowledge - Task 03 - Declarative field control with @mandatory
entry.content:
Among the CDS annotations for declaring constraints when modelling, there are a handful that can be used to drive behaviour and restrictions in Fiori UIs, by causing the generation of appropriate annotations in the service's metadata.

One of these annotations is @mandatory, and this causes an OData annotation to be generated on the target that corresponds to the element annotated in CDS:

<Annotations Target="...">
  <Annotation Term="..." EnumMember="..." />
</Annotations>

The OData vocabulary Common contains the term FieldControl. In the <Annotation ... /> element that is generated, there's an attribute Term which points to this term, and a second attribute EnumMember which contains the fully qualified value for that term, a value which represents "mandatory".

What is that fully qualified value?

This is a question from the June Developer Challenge on CAP Knowledge. And don't forget: always submit your answer as a hash, on its own - read the Taking part section of the intro post for more info. At the end of today, this question will be updated with links to further reading on this topic.

entry.title: **Subject: Unable to connect Eclipse BW Modeling Tools / ABAP Development Tools to SAP BW/4HANA 2023
entry.content:
Hi everyone,

I deployed a SAP BW/4HANA 2023 SP04 Trial appliance through SAP Cloud Appliance Library (CAL) on Microsoft Azure.

Environment

  • SAP BW/4HANA 2023 SP04 Trial
  • SAP CAL deployment on Azure
  • Eclipse with:
    • BW Modeling Tools
    • ABAP Development Tools (ADT)
  • Accessing the system from a local Windows machine

What I have verified

  1. Appliance status is Active in CAL.
  2. CAL provides a SAP GUI connection with:
    • SID: A4H
    • Instance Number: 00
    • Client: 100
  3. Network connectivity appears to be working:
    • Port 3200 is reachable.
    • Port 44300 is reachable.

Eclipse Configuration

For both BW Project and ABAP Project creation, I am using:

  • Connection Type: Custom Application Server
  • System ID: A4H
  • Application Server:
  • Instance Number: 00

Problem

Both BW Project and ABAP Project fail before authentication with the following error:

Logon to system A4H failed
(reject connection request from <local_machine>:eclipse to <server>:sapdp00)

The error occurs even before user validation.

Additional Information

  • The SAP CAL "Connect" option only downloads a .sap file for SAP GUI.
  • I do not currently have SAP GUI installed(due to license that we don't have), so testing via SAP GUI is not yet possible.
  • The error is the same regardless of the SAP user entered.

Questions

  1. What does "reject connection request ... sapdp00" typically indicate in a BW/4HANA Trial appliance?
  2. Is there any additional SAP profile parameter or dispatcher setting that must be enabled for Eclipse BW Modeling Tools / ADT access?
  3. Is connecting through the public VM IP supported for this appliance, or should Eclipse use a different hostname from the SAP system configuration?
  4. Has anyone successfully connected BW Modeling Tools to the SAP BW/4HANA 2023 Trial appliance from CAL, and if so, what connection method was used?

Any guidance would be greatly appreciated.

entry.title: Re: How can I copy a whole folder/directory within Signav...
entry.content:
Hello 

You cannot copy an entire folder/directory in one step in Signavio Process Manager Explorer.
Signavio currently works at diagram/item level, not folder-level duplication

entry.title: Re: Confirmation of combined Orders in Mill_OC
entry.content:
Hi FHBCK,

1. Confirmation status not reflected to underlying order - Partial Confirmation status does not flow back to Original Order(Child Orders), only when your Combined Order is confirmed you will see the status of the Original Orders will also update. Here the logic is as the Operations are being processed as part of Combined Order hence the Original Order Operations become a dummy operation so all the tracking, Costing etc will be taken care by Combined Order.

2. Regarding the first ten Operations query - For this there is an option of Partial Combination which can be achieved using 'Create Combined Operations' here you will have option to select the Operation of your choice. The Operations should be in a sequence and in case you are selecting Operations from the middle then you will have to first process your Operations from the Original Order which are before the starting Combined Operations. Make sure that the Routing for Operations matches your MM and Order Header configs else you will see different results upon Operation combination.

 

In both Whole and Partial Combination after your Combined Order and Original Orders are Confirmed you can now process your Original Order for activities like GR and then mark the Combined Order as TECO.

entry.title: SAP Business Data Cloud and Datasphere News in May
entry.content:
SAP Business Data Cloud and Datasphere News in May

May was one of those months where everything happened at once. SAPPHIRE Orlando brought a wave of announcements for SAP Business Data Cloud — platform direction, new integrations, and some big bets on enterprise AI. And while the conference was still in full swing, the product team kept shipping: two solid Datasphere releases packed with useful enhancements for data integration, modeling, and transport.

Below you'll find my top feature picks from the May releases, a summary of the key SAPPHIRE announcements, and the usual roundup of community blogs worth reading.

 

 

My top features in May

SAP Snowflake and BDC Connect for Snowflake

Two closely related announcements that mark a big step for the BDC open partner ecosystem.

SAP Snowflake is now generally available as a solution extension — Snowflake's AI/ML and advanced data engineering capabilities delivered directly through SAP. Full Snowflake Business Critical Edition, provisioned via SAP for Me, with zero-copy access to SAP data products out of the box.

BDC Connect for Snowflake adds bidirectional data sharing on AWS for customers already running Snowflake. Same enrollment approach as BDC Connect for Databricks — data products flow in both directions, business semantics and governance intact.

Run Replication Flows with Delta Load at Scheduled Times

Long-awaited. You can now schedule replication flows that use delta-enabled objects by setting Delta Load Run to "At Scheduled Time". The flow processes all available delta records and completes cleanly after each run. No more workarounds.

Bulk Assignment of Schedule Owner for Task Chains

You can now assign a schedule owner to multiple task chains at once — no more one-by-one configuration. A small change with a big impact for anyone managing larger environments with many scheduled jobs.

Analyze the Lineage Graph of a View with Runtime Metrics

The Runtime Metrics tool now lets you analyze the full lineage graph of a view, including all underlying source objects — not just the view in isolation. Much clearer visibility into where performance bottlenecks actually live.

Transport Runtime Settings for Flows and Views

Transformation flow runtime settings — HANA run modes, batch configurations, Spark application settings — can now be included in transport packages. The same applies to view persistency runtime settings in the Data Integration Monitor. One transport, consistent behavior across dev, test, and production.

Variables in Analytic Models

When using an analytic model as a source for another analytic model, you can now customize its variables — rename them, change default values, use them in restricted measures and calculations in the consuming model. A real flexibility gain for anyone building layered analytic models.

SAP Analytics Cloud Q2 2026 — Top 5 Features

A quick look at the QRC2 2026 SAC release Orla_C put together a great video walkthrough covering the top 5:

  • Asymmetric Reporting
  • Composite Versioning
  • Job Monitor Enhancements
  • Decoupled Data Panel
  • Recent & Favorites in open/save dialogs

➡️  Watch it here: https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=0U7xc_8WN2I

 
 

 

SAPPHIRE 2026

My personal highlight at SAPPHIRE Orlando 🌴

This year I had the privilege of opening the SAP Business Data Cloud track at SAPPHIRE Orlando with the introductory session on Monday — and the response was something I didn't expect.

The session ended up as the #1 most registered session at SAPPHIRE 2026 overall, with more than 1,800 sign-ups over two sessions. And a lot of them showed up in the room. I'm still a bit speechless. Humbled, and honestly — kind of proud.

Thank you to everyone who registered, attended, or just followed along from afar. The energy in that room made it feel like more than a tech talk.

 

What's New in SAP Business Data Cloud at SAPPHIRE

SAPPHIRE 2026 brought a wave of announcements for SAP Business Data Cloud. Here's a quick summary of what was announced.

SAP Business AI Platform — SAP BTP, SAP Business Data Cloud, and SAP Business Transformation Management are converging into a single unified platform for building, contextualizing, and governing enterprise AI. At its core sits the SAP Knowledge Graph, which maps business entities, processes, and relationships across your SAP landscape.

SAP HANA Cloud natively in BDC — HANA Cloud becomes a core component of BDC, adding a unified in-memory engine for transactional, analytical, and multi-model workloads — alongside the existing SAP Databricks and SAP Snowflake integrations.

MDG and Reltio in BDC — SAP Master Data Governance is now a component of BDC, ensuring data products are verified and governance-aligned. The Reltio acquisition is complete and will be brought directly into BDC as well, adding multi-domain master data management with AI-based entity resolution and Model Context Protocol support for multi-agent workflows.

Joule Agents in the data fabric — Data products, analytical models, and planning structures can be created through natural language. SAP Analytics Cloud story generation is part of this too.

BDC Connect for Amazon Athena — Bi-directional zero-copy integration between BDC and Amazon Athena, extending the existing Snowflake and Databricks integrations. General availability planned for H2 2026.

👉I covered all of this in a dedicated recap video 

 

My favorite blogs in May

@GregBotticchio covers a genuinely useful new feature in SAP Analytics Cloud Q2 2026: the Asymmetric Reporting Layout lets you design tables where different sections follow different structures — actuals at monthly level, forecast from a cut-off date, budget at quarterly level — all in a single table, no workarounds, no duplicated objects. If you've ever built three separate tables just to answer one business question, this one's for you.
🔗 Asymmetric Reporting Layout

@yoramfriedman breaks down a structural problem that doesn't get talked about enough: the place where business context lives can't run agents, and the place that can run agents doesn't have the context. Using S/4HANA as the example, he shows how meaning — contract terms, credit history, priority rules, semantic layers — stays behind when you extract data to a generic lake. A sharp read that explains the core problem SAP Business Data Cloud is designed to solve.
🔗 The Catch-22 of Most Business AI

@Gaurish-Dessai announces something worth knowing about: a new advanced learning journey and certification for SAP Data Architects, covering data strategy for the agentic AI era, architectural frameworks like TOGAF and DAMA-DMBOK, and SAP BDC integrations with Databricks, Snowflake, Fabric, and BigQuery. If data architecture is your game, this is your next step.
🔗 New SAP Data Architecture Learning Journey & SAP Certified Data Architect Certification is now Live

@cecilysorenson covers the latest Discovery Center updates — including a dedicated SAP BDC Service Catalog with missions, roadmaps, and transparent pricing, plus an AI Maturity Calculator developed with IDC. One central place to go from initial BDC exploration to full adoption, with practical guidance for managing SAP and non-SAP data.
🔗 What's New in SAP Discovery Center: From Inspiration to Guidance

@kurzdo93 traces the evolution of enterprise data architecture from the data warehouse era through the Lakehouse to what's needed today: a knowledge core that gives AI agents the business context to actually act, not just answer. He uses SAP Business Data Cloud to show how lake storage, intelligent compute (HANA Cloud, Databricks, Snowflake), semantic data products, and Joule agents come together as a business data fabric. A well-structured read if you want to understand the architectural "why" behind BDC.
🔗 AI has outgrown legacy data architectures like SAP BW — is your stack ready to keep up?



More blogs about SAP BDC and Datasphere to check out 👇

Please try:

if (payload.PICTURE) {
  const base64 = payload.PICTURE.includes(",")
    ? payload.PICTURE.split(",")[1]
    : payload.PICTURE;

  const base64Buffer = Buffer.from(base64, "base64");
  payload.PICTURE = base64Buffer.toString('binary');
}
payload.MATNR = "000004711";

entry.title: Re: Change of billing material determined in project billing
entry.content:
Hi Johannes,

the use case you are describing is a common case in the professional service scenario.

As the activity types are (usually) in a 1:1 relationship with the materials the roles in the work package already represent the levels sold to the customer, in your case the senior. To get the correct cost rates of the employee, in your case junior, posted to the project the service cost level is the solution to use. The service cost level is an attribute to the HR master of the employee. If you maintain a cost rate for the specific service cost levels in addition to the ones per activity type, the service cost level ones are more specific and are then used when the hour is posted by the employee with service cost level "Junior", independent on the activity type. If you are staffing the resource this cost rate is also reflected in project planning.

It is technically not possible to include non-billable hours into your invoice, to get them into the project billing you'd need to change the time entry in the source system from non-billable to billable.

Best regards,

David

entry.title: Re: A Comprehensive Guide to SAP BTP Certifications [2025 Updated]
entry.content:
I see your point, and I don't disagree that solid fundamentals in ABAP, OData, and business processes are essential.

However, my concern is more fundamental: Are we (as customers/developers) buying into a technology stack that primarily serves SAP's own business goals—like cloud lock-in and subscription revenue—rather than solving real customer pain points?

I've seen too many cases where SAP pushes a "modern" solution (ABAP Cloud, CAP, Build Work Zone) that looks great on paper, but in reality, customers are hesitant to adopt because the value proposition is unclear and the costs are high.

So my question isn't "should I get certified before learning the basics?" It's: "Is this technology path even worth investing in at all, given the market's cold response?"

I'm genuinely curious to hear from others who have implemented these new SAP technologies in real projects. What was the business outcome?

entry.title: Re: Hotspot TU using TU_NUM_EXT
entry.content:
Hello @Immanuel1993 

What do you mean by the note can't be implemented in SNOTE?

The note 3341498 - Hotspot for Transportation Unit in EWM monitor for inbound/outbound deliveries provides correction instructions for SCMEWM and S/4HANA:

The corrections are included in the following support packages:

The note can be implemented in SNOTE unless your system is already on one of those packages or higher.

Also consider the manual activities of the note. You need to run the attached Z-report manually to update the /SCWM/TSRVC_MAP table with a new record, where:

  • SERVICE = TU
  • KEYFIELD = TU_NUM_EXT

I'd suggest the following:

  • check your package level to make sure the corrections of the note are already implemented and
  • check the content of /SCWM/TSRVC_MAP in SE16 to make sure the record for TU_NUM_EXT is there.

Best regards

Dominik Tylczynski

entry.title: A Big Leap Beyond C4C: Changes in Communication Systems & Authentication in Sales Cloud V2 - Part 1
entry.content:
Introduction

This blog explores the changes to the Communication System in SAP Sales/Service Cloud Version 2 (SSCV2) that lay the foundation for integrating with external systems, whether through direct API connectivity, middleware-mediated scenarios via SAP Integration Suite, or side-by-side extensibility on SAP Business Technology Platform (BTP). It also covers the authentication mechanisms available in SCV2 and how they compare and, in many ways, contrast with what SAP Cloud for Customer (C4C) offered.

Having worked in the SAP CX space for over 12 years across SAP C4C, SAP Field Service Management (FSM), SAP Customer Data Cloud (Gigya), and SAP Marketing Cloud, our recent implementation on SAP Sales/Service Cloud V2 gained some significant learnings, particularly around how the integration and extensibility model has fundamentally evolved from the C4C era.

The following abbreviations are used throughout this blog:

SAP C4C - SAP Cloud for Customer

SAP SSCV2 - SAP Sales/Service Cloud V2 (latest version of the product)

 

What is a Communication System?

In both SAP C4C and Sales/Service Cloud V2, a Communication System represents an external logical system instance, such as a SAP BTP subaccount, an ERP back-end, or a middleware platform that exchanges data with Sales Cloud. Think of it as the identity card you issue to a partner system before it can walk in the door.

 

SAP C4C Baseline — What We're Moving Away From

SAP Cloud for Customer's integration model has the authentication methods that support the administrators to have two choices:

  • Basic Authentication

  • SSL / X.509 Certificates

Why this matters now: Basic Authentication is being phased out across SAP's landscape. SAP SuccessFactors and SAP BTP have already announced sunset timelines for Basic Auth on their APIs. Organisations must migrate their integrations to OAuth 2.0 before this deadline to prevent disruptions.

 

SAP Sales/Service Cloud V2 Communication System — A Richer Model

SAP Sales/Service Cloud V2 provides a future-proof solution with multiple options to the SAP partners/customers.

When you create a Communication System in Sales Cloud V2 (via Settings → Integration → Communication Systems), you'll immediately notice it's structured around two distinct directions, each with its own authentication model.

 

 

Deleted Communication Systems

A key improvement in SAP SSCV2 is the ability to restore the Communication System after it is deleted.

An Administrator can delete a Communication System, and it is moved to the Deleted Communication Systems rather than being removed from the system entirely, unlike in SAP C4C.

Steps to Delete a Communication System

  1. Change the Status of the Communication System from Active to Inactive

  1. Click the Delete button

  2. Deleted Communication System visible in the section below:

  3. Remaining Days shows the number of days until the System removes it from the Deletion table in SAP SSCV2. Clicking the Undo button will make the Communication System Inactive

  4. Clicking the Delete button will permanently delete it from SAP SSCV2.

The retention time for the Deleted Communication System is 60 days. After 60 days, it is permanently deleted and cannot be recovered.

 

Authentication Methods: Comparison between SAP C4C and SAP SSCV2

The table below lists the authentication methods available for Inbound and Outbound connectivity with SAP SSCV2.

The ideal scenario would be to integrate SAP SSCV2 with SAP Integration Suite and use SAP BTP (Kyma or Cloud Foundry) for extensibility.

 

DirectionTypeSAP C4CSAP SSCV2Notable Changes

Inbound

Basic Authentication (Username / Password)

Business user controlled by Business Roles assigned & Technical User authentication.

Technical User authentication is controlled by the Technical Roles assigned. Clear separation between a Technical User and a business user

Improved access control on Technical User and Technical Roles in SAP SSCV2

Inbound

SSL Client Certificates

Key pair certificate created in C4C, middleware, or an external system.

Key pair certificate created in a middleware or an external system.

Remains the same with a different user experience in SAP SSCV2

Outbound

Basic Authentication (Username / Password)

A username and password provided by a middleware or an external system that has access to the respective API.

A username and password provided by a middleware or an external system that has access to the respective API.

Remains the same with a different user experience in SAP SSCV2

Outbound

SSL Certificate Authentication

SAP trusted, or a 3rd party trusted Key pair certificate downloadable from Outbound Communication from C4C

Keypair certificate created in BTP, middleware with a passphrase uploaded in SSCV2.

A key pair certificate can only be created in external systems and uploaded to SAP SSCV2

Outbound

OAuth 2.0 Client Credentials Grant

NA

The Client fetches the access token and passes the token in the subsequent step to get access to the resources

New feature in SAP SSCV2

Outbound

OAuth 2.0 SAML Bearer Assertion

NA

Principal propagation is achieved through the OAuth2 SAML Bearer assertion flow. The sender app uses a custom identity provider that also acts as a SAML token issuer.

New feature in SAP SSCV2

Outbound

OAuth 2.0 Token Exchange

NA

Get a new token for an existing token with a different scope and audience, and pass the new token to access the resources.

New feature in SAP SSCV2



Recommendation: Don't use the Basic authentication method in productive tenants for security reasons

How to choose the right Authentication method

Choosing the right authentication method in SAP SSCV2 is not purely a technical decision; it also has implications for security governance, audit compliance, and the operational overhead your basis and integration teams will carry throughout the life of the implementation. The right choice depends on three major things:

  • Who or what is calling SAP SSCV2

  • Whether a human user's identity needs to be traceable in the system

  • Whether middleware sits between the caller and SAP SSCV2.

The table below summarises the recommendations based on our experience by scenario:

 

Scenario

Recommended Authentication Method

Pros

Cons

Direct API Call using Basic Auth, where no possibility of maintaining the Certificate or OAuth

Basic Auth (Technical User/Technical Role)

  • Familiar, quick to set up, and works without any additional infrastructure.

  • Appropriate for development sandboxes and early-phase integration testing

  • SAP SSCV2 provides better control through features such as Technical User and Technical Roles, which provide granular access to APIs.

  • Credentials are long-lived, shared between systems, and must be rotated manually, all of which introduces risk as the integration landscape grows.

  • Basic Authentication, in most cases, should be treated as a starting point, not a target state.

For integrations running through SAP Cloud Integration

Client Certificate Authentication

  • The Communication System in SCV2 is configured with the Integration Suite tenant's certificate on the inbound side.

  • SAP SSCV2 uses the BTP-generated certificate from the service instance for outbound calls into Integration Suite iFlows

  • Similar to integration patterns with SAP C4C

  • The certificate validity period is visible and auditable.

  • Initial Setup is required, and technical knowledge of certificate work is required

 

For SAP BTP side-by-side extensions

OAuth 2.0 with SAML Bearer Assertion

  • Passes the context of the logged-in user from and to SAP SSCV2. This is a pattern that enabled Principal Propagation

  • This is a new feature that was not available with C4C

  • An application embedded in SAP SSCV2 as a Mashup that runs in SAP BTP (created from SAP Build or apps running in Cloud Foundry or Kyma) will capture the actual logged-in user in the change logs in SAP SSCV2 rather than a generic technical user.

  • Since the logged-in user context is being passed, the logged-in user Business Role must have sufficient access to the API calls made in the application. Business Role must be managed in such a way

  • SAP Cloud Identity Service is required as a prerequisite.

  • The user should be available in the BTP subaccount has the necessary roles in BTP if the application is calling an integration flow.

 

Server-Server Calls (Ex: Custom Entity Service, Direct Non-SAP integrations)

OAuth 2.0 – Client Credentials

  • This is used to access an external service directly from the backend of Sales/Service Cloud V2 by using a dedicated Client ID.

  • Scopes can be configured to restrict what that token can access, which is especially valuable when integrating with systems outside the SAP landscape.

  • User context is not passed to the external service

 

In practice, the final choice of authentication method will be shaped by factors specific to each client's landscape and internal security policies.

Key considerations include:

  • Custom Identity Provider (IdP) availability

  • Enterprise security standards

  • Certificate lifecycle management

  • Existing integration platform capabilities

 

In my next blog, I will walk through the step-by-step configuration of the recommended authentication methods in SAP Sales/Service Cloud V2, covering Basic Auth using a Technical User, Client Certificate setup for SAP Integration Suite and OAuth 2.0 SAML Bearer Assertion for SAP BTP side-by-side extensions.

Thanks, 

Adi

entry.title: What We Learned Hosting a SAP Document AI Workshop
entry.content:
Introduction

Some weeks ago we had a hands-on event focused on SAP Document AI and enterprise document automation. We had planned it to be just a technical workshop, yet the attendees wasted no time in telling us all kinds of complications they were experiencing at the time. Soon, we were talking about operational processes, workflow complexity, and the increasing importance of AI in enterprise environments.

When we were planning the workshop we did not just want to show people a product. Our goal was to create a space where participants could engage directly with actual document processing situations, as well as investigate automation options that might enhance their routine workflows. I mean, you can't have an opinion about something you haven't used or experienced yourself.

During the session attendees had the opportunity to experiment with document classification, extraction models, workflow routing and automation processes related to larger SAP workflows.

As the Hackathon went on, the conversations naturally shifted toward some of the operational challenges many companies are currently facing. Although SAP Document AI was the focus, there was also room for repetitive manual work, fragmented validation processes, inconsistent document formats, and the difficulty of scaling document-heavy operations across teams and business units.

 

What the workshop was about

The workshop included product exploration and practical activities, simulating real enterprise scenarios. Rather than merely attend presentations or demos, participants were able to directly engage with workflows, classifiers, extraction schemas, and automation processes.

The event provided participants with insights into how SAP Document AI can support invoicing processes, free-form documents, workflow routing, and document classification.

 

One exercise that participants seemed to find particularly useful was the ROI calculator we brought to the workshop. While most of the day focused on workflows and automation scenarios, the calculator helped ground those discussions in something every organization cares about: business impact. It gave attendees a way to estimate how document automation could affect their own operations and helped move the conversation from technical possibilities to practical outcomes.

I think we were really taken aback by the diversity of perspectives in the room. People came from different industries and business environments, and brought very different operational realities and use cases to the discussion.

We were surprised that many of the issues being discussed were incredibly similar. Within all industries, companies had a lot of the same challenges around managing documents, approvals, integrations and keeping processes consistent. 

While working on practical document-processing scenarios, it became evident that the challenges most companies face go far beyond document extraction. As operations expand across multiple departments and teams, many organizations are still faced with fragmented workflows, repetitive manual validations, inconsistent document formats and processes that are becoming harder to manage.

And the workshop also reinforced something we often see when working with SAP technologies: real conversations happen once people can interact directly with a solution. The hackathon became much more practical and grounded after participants started to set up schemas, test classifiers and experiment with workflows. Rather than considering AI as something far away or theoretical, attendees began to consider how these solutions could realistically be integrated into their own operational environments.

Another important conclusion we arrived at was the importance of integration for successful AI adoption. Precise extraction models are crucial, no one will deny that, but they are just one part of the process. Companies also require workflows capable of adapting to operational changes, handling variations between document types, and collaborating effectively over time with existing systems.

Some Reflections

One of the most gratifying aspects of the workshop was to look back and see how quickly participants started to relate the exercises to their own organizations. Throughout the day the conversation evolved easily from examples under discussion to real business processes, internal priorities and potential next steps. Watching that transition in real time was a reminder of how the value of these sessions often goes far beyond the tech itself.

The workshop was also a great opportunity for us to hear directly from organizations that are facing different operational challenges, and to get a better understanding of how they are approaching document automation and AI adoption within their SAP landscapes.

Some of the most valuable insights came not only from the practical tasks but from the conversations that took place around them. The people who came had different perspectives, experiences and business contexts and we learnt from each other. The workshop was a valuable experience for all involved. As for me, I’m already looking forward to the next one, whether that’s as a host, a participant, or simply someone eager to keep learning.

entry.title: What's New in the SAP Green Ledger Analytical Dashboard in Q2 2026
entry.content:
New Here?

The SAP Green Ledger Analytical Dashboard is an SAP Analytics Cloud story that puts greenhouse gas (GHG) emissions and financial data on the same canvas: KPIs, trend charts, a CO2e flow statement, and a financial statement with CO2e quantities. It's built on top of SAP Datasphere (data layer) and SAP Analytics Cloud (presentation layer), and ships as ready-to-import business content.

For the full tour of pages, KPIs, filters, and how the calculations work, see Analyze Carbon Data Alongside Financial Data | SAP Help Portal in the SAP Green Ledger documentation. The setup walkthrough lives in the Install SAP Green Ledger Analytical Dashboard | SAP Help Portal.

 

What's new in Q2/2026?

Release time: May 18, 2026. SAC content version 1.5.1, Datasphere content version 1.5.1.

 

A cleaner look with the Horizon theme

The dashboard now uses the Horizon theme, aligning it visually with the rest of the SAP Fiori experience. 

 

Units spelled out, not abbreviated

  • KG → Kilogram
  • TO → Ton

 

New WBS Element filter

You can now slice carbon and financial data by Work Breakdown Structure (WBS) element, so project-level analysis is finally possible directly in the dashboard.

 

Sharper KPI logic for Costs of Goods Sold

The semantic tag used to calculate Costs of Goods Sold changed from RECO_COGS to COGS. This makes the calculation simpler and aligns it with standard financial tagging.

 

CO2e Flow Statement: all filters available

All filters now apply to the CO2e Flow Statement on page 3, so the same filtering you use elsewhere in the dashboard works here too.

 

Cleaner page-level filters

  • End Period and Comparison End Period moved to page 4 as page-level filters.

  • Fiscal Year Period now defaults to the current fiscal year, so the dashboard is useful the moment you open it. 

 

German translation now available

The dashboard is now available in German. You can edit your preferred language in Settings from the shell bar. To enable it, change the display language. For more information, see Edit Your User Settings.

 

New how-to guide: updating content

For customers already running an earlier version of the business content, the new Update Existing Business Content | SAP Help Portal topic provides a structured path to the latest release. 

 

Troubleshooting

For known issues and resolutions, see Troubleshooting. If the issue persists, contact technical support.

 

What's next

We'd love to hear from you:

  • Which dashboard pages do you use most?
  • Are there filters or KPIs you wish were there?
  • Did the documentation save you a support ticket, or expose a step we should clarify?

Leave your comment below and let us know what's working for you, what you'd like to see next, and how we can keep making the SAP Green Ledger experience better!

entry.title: SLT Rule Assignment – “Field LAST_ACTION_CD does not exist in table despite field being added
entry.content:
Hello experts,

I am currently working on SLT trigger-based replication (ECC → target) and trying to implement a Rule Assignment with a custom include to derive a field LAST_ACTION_CD. My requirement is to change the operation flag so  I can distinguish between archived records and physically deleted records since both records have operation flag  = "D".

However, I am facing an issue where SLT throws the following errors during source setup:

"Source setup failed due to the following error: Cannot save filter settings for object VBAK Cannot save filter settings for object VBAK Field LAST_ACTION_CD does not exist in table VBAK"

What I have done

1. Table Enhancement in SLT

In LTRS → Table Settings → VBAK → Table Structure:

  • I added a new field:
    Field Name: LAST_ACTION_CD
Data Type: CHAR
Length: 1
  • The field is visible in: Modified Table Structure as shown in the image

2. Rule Assignment

In Rule Assignment, I created:

  • Rule Type: Field-Related

  • Assignment Target: LAST_ACTION_CD

  • Implementation: Custom include program

  • Import Parameters:

    I_P1 = HARD_DELETE_BUSS
I_P2 = HARD_DELETE_ARCH

3. Custom Include

I implemented a custom include (Z_*_LAST_ACTION_CD_A)  using SE80 to populate LAST_ACTION_CD based on IUUC_OPERAT_FLAG.

Despite all these and regeneration with program IUUC_REDEFINE_DB_TRIGGERS , I am still getting error in datasphere replication flow that the field does not exist. 

How do I fix it and does anyone has experience in changing the operation flag. 

We are on SAP ECC and DMIS 2018

Thanks,

Bhat

entry.title: Re: Managing Pre-approvals using Attendance Quota in SAP SuccessFactors
entry.content:
@PetraRana 

Please find below the answers to your questions:

1. Can EXTRA time type be configured to deduct Attendance Quota?

Ans: Yes EXTRA time type can be configure in Attendance Quota. I have added a screenshot where I used the EXTRA time type to add this to a time type group

It works for all time valuations (Route input based on Decision, Filter Input Groups and Aggregate Input Groups and Split) where Attendance Quota variables are used.

2. Can the Quota Limit be calculated by system automatically? For example we have use case when we have annual overtime limit which has to be prorated for new starters. Or can the attendance quota only be created by Employees on need basis based on the template configured by the Administrator?

Ans: Quota limit cannot be calculated by the system automatically. Attendance Quota currently can only be created for employees for your use case. Prorated overtime limit will required to be calculated and assigned to the Quota Limit field for the employee. It can also be configured by Administrators without the need of a workflow.

3. Can Attendance Quotas be generated in mass for employees without the employee having to request it? Our use case is to allocate the overtime limit at the beginning of calendar year or on employee hire automatically without the need for each employee requesting the attendance quota at the beginning of the year.

Ans: Attendance Quota can be generated in mass without employee having to request by uploading via Import and Export Data. You could as well use the Global Variable Value to set the default value and override when the employee has a changed value. This would also work without having to create for all the employees.

entry.title: External Vendor PO GR via MIGO for EWM Plants (WO IBD)
entry.content:
Many occasions we need flexible way to receive the external vendor POs into the warehouse (EWM) without creating inbound delivery due to lack ASN confirmation from vendors or other operational issues. 

Recently i learned about Synchronous Goods Movement Process which allows external vendor POs can be received vi MIGO without an inbound delivery to be created 

Pre-Requisites:

1. Synchronous Goods Movement functionality is activated

2. POs are created without confirmation control key

3. Pack Specs are created with correct condition type (this step is only required if you want to build the HU for label printing etc).

Configuration:

1. SIMG –> SCM Extended Warehouse Management–> Extended Warehouse Management –> Cross-Process Settings -> Handling Units -> Package Building –> Define Package Building Profile.

2. SIMG –> SCM Extended Warehouse Management –> Extended Warehouse Management –> Interfaces –> ERP Integration –> Goods Movements –> Activate Synchronous Goods Movement Posting.

3. SIMG –> SCM Extended Warehouse Management –> Extended Warehouse Management –> Interfaces –> ERP Integration –> Goods Movements –> Make Settings for Synchronous Goods Movement Posting

4.  SIMG –> SCM Extended Warehouse Management –> Extended Warehouse Management –> Cross-Process Settings –> Warehouse Task -> Define Warehouse Process Type

Note : make sure, Auto WT creation at GR is active

5.  SIMG –> SCM Extended Warehouse Management –> Extended Warehouse Management –> Cross-Process Settings –> Warehouse Task -> Determine Warehouse Process Type.

Note : there are two process indicators available for Vendor PO GR related (Packed & Unpacked)

Additionally, for the respective Storage type, we need to maintain the storage type search sequence and Storage bin assignment.

Testing Process: 

1. PO Created without confirmation control key

2. MIGO 

3. WT display at MIGO

 

for reference purpose, i am sharing the pack spec which i have created for the above material 

Conclusion:

With this process, we have observed that, external vendor POs can be received into EWM locations without an inbound delivery, and it simplifies goods receiving process and also with reference to palletization data we can build the HUs and print the labels.

 

Thank you to all my mentors!
Hari Sivvala

https://learning.sap.com/courses/basic-customizing-in-sap-s-4hana-ewm/posting-goods-receipt-for-embedded-ewm

entry.title: Changing the unit of measure during RF packing in SAP EWM
entry.content:
Introduction

In material and engineering manufacturing industries, products are often managed and sold in more than one unit of measure. A typical example is steel bars: stocked and sold by length (1 m, 2 m, 3 m, etc.), yet some customers require delivery in KG, or warehouse teams need to repack stock into KG before outbound shipment.

SAP Extended Warehouse Management (EWM) supports this scenario as a standard operation through the RF/mobile device packing transaction - no custom development required. This blog walks through the end-to-end configuration and testing steps.

Business scenario

  • Product: Steel Bar
  • Base / ordering UoM: M (metres)
  • Alternative UoM: KG

Requirement: During outbound packing on the RF device, the operator repacks a quantity of steel bars into a destination HU expressed in KG

Define Unit of Measurement Attributes (prerequisite)

This is a critical step that is easy to overlook. For the UoM switch to work during RF packing, the alternative unit must be flagged as Stock-Specific UoM at the warehouse level.

IMG path: EWM > Master Data > Product > Define Unit of Measurement Attributes

Define Unit of Measurement Attributes - KG flagged as Stock-Specific UoM for warehouse

Create Exception code

IMG path: EWM > Cross-Process settings > Exception Handling > Create Exception Codes

  • Warehouse:XXXX
  • Exception Code: AUOM (or any 4-character key, e.g. ZUOM, XAUO)
  • Description: Change AUoM

     

    Assign the business context

    Navigate to child menu Define Exception Code > Details and set the following:

    • Business Context: PIM – Packing/Unpacking Products (Internal Movement)
    • Execution Step: A0 (background)
    • Exception Code Profile: assign only if restricting to a specific user group


Exception Code details - Business Context: PIM Packing/Unpacking, Execution Step: A0

Within the exception code configuration, maintain the process parameter for differences. Set Difference Category to blank - this ensures no stock difference is posted to a bin, account, or delivery when the UoM is switched. The system converts the quantity using the material master conversion factor.

Assign Internal Exception Code to your Exception Code

Navigate to:  IMG > EWM > Cross-process setting > Exception Handling > Assign Internal Exception Codes to Exception Codes

Create a new entry mapping your external exception code to SAP's delivered internal exception code AUOM - "Change Quantity and/or Unit". This is the link that allows EWM to trigger the UoM change function automatically when the exception is raised during RF packing. Assign Internal Exception Codes - external code mapped to internal AUOM (Change Quantity and/or Unit)

Testing - RF device

Navigate to: RFUI > Outbound Process > Packing > Manually Repack HU Item

Scan the source HU RF device - scan the source HU containing the steel bars

Select item and switch UoM

The system displays the item (Steel Bar for Export, 3 M). Press SF2 (UoMC) - the UoM field opens and becomes editable. Change to KG and enter the required quantity (e.g. 5 KG). RF device - press SF2 (UoMC) to open the UoM field; item currently shows 3 M


Assign destination HU

Enter the destination HU (pre-created, or create in real time using F4 HUCrt). The screen now confirms AQty = 5 KG with the destination HU and storage bin. 

 RF device - destination HU assigned, quantity confirmed as 5 KG

Result - HU overview

After confirming, the HU overview shows the stock correctly split across both handling units:HU overview - source HU retains remaining metres; destination HU holds 5 KG


Desktop packing transaction

The same UoM conversion is available in the /SCWM/PACK desktop work center (Work Center Pack in Goods Issue) without any additional exception code configuration. The Alt. Unit and Quantity columns are available directly in the packing item list.

 /SCWM/PACK desktop transaction - UoM conversion available with no extra configuration

 

Summary

This standard SAP EWM capability is particularly relevant for industries handling dual-unit materials: steel, aluminium, wire, timber, fabric, bulk chemicals, and similar products.

  • Enable Stock-Specific UoM for the alternative unit in warehouse settings - this is the key prerequisite
  • Configure an exception code mapped to SAP's internal AUOM exception
  • Warehouse operators can switch UoM directly on the RF device during packing
  • No stock discrepancies are posted - the system converts using the material master factor

The same operation is also available in the /SCWM/PACK desktop transaction with no extra config

I hope this guide is helpful. Open for feedback and suggestions - please share your experience or questions in the comments below.

entry.title: SAP Joule for Developers is now available for lower releases of SAP Cloud ERP Private.
entry.content:
SAP Joule for Developers as a Side-by-Side ABAP AI Service Offering for Lower Releases of SAP Cloud ERP Private (SAP S/4HANA Private Cloud Edition)

Joule speaks ABAP, and now it speaks to more systems than ever before.

SAP Joule for Developers, ABAP AI capabilities have already created strong interest across the SAP ecosystem. From customers to partners, one message has been consistent, make these powerful developer capabilities available for lower SAP S/4HANA releases as well.

The feedback was clear, and SAP has delivered.

Joule for Developers is now being extended to SAP S/4HANA Private Cloud 2021 and higher, covering systems on SAP_BASIS 756 and above. This means organizations running earlier private cloud releases can now start benefiting from AI-assisted ABAP development sooner, without waiting for their next major upgrade cycle.

Why side by side architecture?

The introduction of the new ABAP AI side-by-side architecture marks a strategic step toward making AI capabilities more accessible, scalable, and future-ready across diverse SAP landscapes. By decoupling AI services from release dependencies, SAP can more rapidly deliver new capabilities powered by both SAP-provided ABAP AI models and leading third-party LLM providers.

One of the key advantages is simplified adoption. Customers can now enable ABAP AI capabilities through a more streamlined onboarding process using SAP for Me and Central Business Configuration, reducing setup effort while ensuring consistent lifecycle management across environments.

Another major benefit is release-independent access. Instead of being tightly coupled to specific SAP S/4HANA release cycles, customers can consume ABAP AI services side by side with their existing systems. This enables even lower-release Private Edition customers to leverage the latest AI innovations without waiting for a system upgrade.

In short, the side-by-side model is not just an architecture shift, it is an innovation enabler, helping customers adopt ABAP AI faster, more flexibly, and with greater future readiness.

Commercial model & AI Units

SAP Joule for Developers, ABAP AI capabilities are embedded in the Joule Premium package and offered with separate licenses for customers and partners. Pricing follows a consumption-based model, meaning you only pay for actual usage. Customers and partners purchase AI Units and activate the required packages via SAP for Me. Authorization for developer access is managed through Cloud Identity Services, while AI requests are executed and metered in the ABAP AI Hub 2.0 on SAP BTP. 

What is an SAP AI Unit?

SAP AI Units are a form of virtual currency that customers purchase to activate and use premium AI capabilities within SAP Cloud Services. They are consumption-based credits that can be used flexibly across multiple SAP solutions and are not tied to a specific product or line of business. This allows customers to draw from a central pool of AI Units as they use AI-powered features across their SAP landscape. AI Units are purchased annually and expire after 12 months if not used.Customers can track their usage and remaining balance in real time via SAP for Me.

How do I know how many units I should purchase?

SAP provides guidance and estimation tools to help determine the right amount of AI Units. Your SAP Account Executive or Partner can support you in defining the appropriate quantity and help model different usage scenarios. 

How to Purchase the License and activate the packages? 

A.    For Customers

Purchase AI Units using SKU 8019164 via SAP for Me to begin the Premium AI activation process.

B.    For partners

Place the order for cloud test, demo, and development for SAP AI Unit (8019670).  

Configuring the SAP Joule for Developers, ABAP AI Capabilities for Side-by-Side Use (Administrators)

Prerequisites

You are an administrator with full access to SAP for Me.

Procedure

  1. Open SAP for Me and navigate to Portfolio & Products.
     
  2. Select the tab My Product Packages and choose Technology Platform AI Agents and Features.
  3. Select the tab Applications and choose the application SAP Joule for Developers, ABAP AI capabilities.
  4. Now you can Start Provisioning.
    1. Enter the Solution Details.
    2. Select a Cloud Identity Services system.
    3. Select your region.
    4. Choose a Formation Name (the name is mandatory but has no further technical relevance).
    5. Review and finish the setup. The provision process might take few moments to complete. The new solution will achieve the Ready status when all tenants are successfully provisioned and integrations are fully established. Go to the resources tab to view the status of the generated resources.
  5. Activate the feature in SAP Central Business Configuration as described here.
  6. Assign named users through SAP Cloud Identity Services (IAS) to enable access for authorized users.
  7. In the tab Resources you will find a new tenant providing its Main URL (which will lead to the UI of the application).

     



  8. Provide that URL to the developers so they can configure SAP Joule for Developers, ABAP AI capabilities for side-by-side use in ABAP development tools for Eclipse.

Configuring the SAP Joule for Developers, ABAP AI Capabilities in ABAP System for Side-by-Side Use (For Developers)

Prerequisites

Your SAP administrator provides you a URL that refers to the SAP BTP.

Configure the SAP Joule for Developers, ABAP AI capabilities for Specific ABAP Projects

To use the latest SAP Joule for Developers, ABAP AI capabilities in specific ABAP projects in your ADT client.

  1. In the Project Explorer, select the relevant ABAP project and Choose Properties from the context menu
  2. Select ABAP Development Joule .To make the input available, select the Enable project-specific settings checkbox.
  3. Beneath the Use Joule side-by-side radio button, enter the URL to the subscription of the central BTP service on the SAP BTP.
    Optional: Choose the Check Connection button to control if the connection works.


  4. Confirm with Apply.
  5. You can now use the SAP Joule for Developers, ABAP AI capabilities side-by-side in the selected ABAP project.
